question: You are building a customer support agent that uses Claude to resolve tickets. Your agentic loop checks whether to continue iterating. A junior developer proposes checking the response text for phrases like 'I have completed the task' to determine loop termination. What is wrong with this approach?

options.text: The response text may not always contain that phrase even when Claude is done, and stop_reason is the authoritative signal for loop termination
Checking the text is valid but should be combined with checking the tool_use block count for reliability
The response text is encrypted and cannot be parsed directly by the client
Claude never produces natural language text alongside tool_use blocks, so the text field will always be empty

explanation: The agentic loop is keyed on the stop_reason field, not on parsing response text. The loop continues ONLY while stop_reason == 'tool_use' and exits on any other value (end_turn, max_tokens, stop_sequence, pause_turn, refusal). Checking text for completion phrases is unreliable because Claude may phrase completion differently each time, and stop_reason is the single authoritative decision point designed for this purpose.

why_others_wrong.b: Checking tool_use block count is also not the correct approach. The stop_reason field is the single authoritative signal  --  it's specifically designed to indicate whether the loop should continue.
why_others_wrong.c: Response text is returned as plain text in the API response and is fully readable by the client. There is no encryption.
why_others_wrong.d: Claude CAN produce natural language text alongside tool_use blocks in the same response  --  both text and tool_use content blocks can appear together in the content array.

doc_reference.quote: The loop exits on any other stop reason... The loop continues ONLY while stop_reason == 'tool_use'. Any other value exits the loop.

question: Your financial operations agent receives a response with stop_reason: 'pause_turn' after executing multiple web searches for market data. What is the correct way to handle this in your client-side loop?

options.text: Extract the partial results from the response and restart the loop from scratch with a new conversation
Re-send the conversation including the paused response as-is in a subsequent request to let Claude continue
Send a tool_result block for each server_tool_use block that lacks a corresponding result
Treat it as an error and terminate the loop with an error message to the user

explanation: When stop_reason is 'pause_turn', the server-side tool loop has hit its iteration limit (default 10). The correct handling is to pass the paused response back as-is in a subsequent API request, optionally modifying content before continuing, and preserving the same tools in the continuation request. The paused response may contain server_tool_use blocks without corresponding server_tool_result blocks  --  this is expected.

why_others_wrong.a: Restarting from scratch would waste all the work done in the paused turn. The correct approach is to continue the existing conversation by passing the paused response back.
why_others_wrong.c: You must NEVER construct tool_result blocks for server-executed tools (those with srvtoolu_ prefix). Server tools are handled entirely by Anthropic's servers. The paused response may intentionally have incomplete server tool results.
why_others_wrong.d: pause_turn is not an error  --  it indicates the server-side loop reached its iteration limit and needs to be continued. Treating it as an error would lose all progress made so far.

doc_reference.quote: Continue the conversation: Pass the paused response back as-is in a subsequent request. The paused response may contain server_tool_use blocks without corresponding server_tool_result blocks  --  this is expected.

question: You are analyzing the behavior of an agentic loop in a code generation pipeline. The loop iterates correctly on tool_use responses but occasionally hangs when Claude returns stop_reason: 'max_tokens'. Which analysis best explains the issue?

options.text: The loop only checks for stop_reason == 'end_turn' to exit, missing the max_tokens case which should also terminate the loop
The max_tokens response always contains incomplete tool_use blocks that need to be retried
The max_tokens stop reason requires sending a special continuation token before the loop can exit
The loop should convert max_tokens responses into tool_use responses by injecting a synthetic tool call

explanation: The canonical agentic loop continues ONLY while stop_reason == 'tool_use'. Any other stop_reason  --  including end_turn, max_tokens, stop_sequence, pause_turn, and refusal  --  should exit the loop. If the loop only checks for end_turn as the exit condition instead of treating tool_use as the only continue condition, it will miss other exit reasons like max_tokens and hang.

why_others_wrong.b: While max_tokens CAN result in incomplete tool_use blocks, the correct handling is to exit the loop and optionally retry with higher max_tokens  --  not to assume tool_use blocks are always present.
why_others_wrong.c: There is no 'continuation token' concept for max_tokens. The response may be truncated, and you can retry with a higher max_tokens limit, but the loop should exit first and handle it as a separate concern.
why_others_wrong.d: Injecting synthetic tool calls is not a valid pattern. The loop should simply exit on any stop_reason other than tool_use, and the truncated response should be handled appropriately.

doc_reference.quote: The loop continues ONLY while stop_reason == 'tool_use'. Any other value exits the loop.

question: You are reviewing a developer productivity agent that uses both client-side and server-side tools. When examining the API responses, you notice two different ID prefixes on tool use blocks. Which statement correctly describes the significance of these prefixes?

options.text: Both prefixes indicate client-executed tools, but srvtoolu_ marks tools that require elevated permissions
toolu_ is for synchronous tools and srvtoolu_ is for asynchronous tools that return results later via webhooks
toolu_ prefix indicates deprecated tools and srvtoolu_ indicates current-generation tools
toolu_ prefix is used for tool_use blocks (client-executed) and srvtoolu_ for server_tool_use blocks (server-executed)  --  you only send tool_result for toolu_ prefixed blocks

explanation: The ID prefix distinguishes who must handle execution. tool_use blocks with the toolu_ prefix are client-executed  --  your application must run them and return a tool_result. server_tool_use blocks with the srvtoolu_ prefix are executed by Anthropic's servers  --  the result appears immediately in the same assistant turn, and you must NEVER construct a tool_result for them.

why_others_wrong.a: srvtoolu_ does not indicate elevated permissions. It indicates the tool runs on Anthropic's servers rather than in your client application.
why_others_wrong.b: Server-executed tools are not asynchronous webhooks. Their results appear immediately in the same assistant turn as the server_tool_use block, already completed by the time you receive the response.
why_others_wrong.c: The prefixes have nothing to do with tool deprecation. They distinguish the execution model: client-side vs server-side.

doc_reference.quote: server_tool_use block id uses the srvtoolu_ prefix (vs toolu_ for client)... By the time you see them, execution is already complete.

question: A monitoring agent frequently runs into infinite loops when Claude keeps calling tools without converging on an answer. You need to evaluate which combination of loop guards would be most appropriate for a production deployment. Which approach provides the best defense?

options.text: Implement maximum iteration count, token budget tracking, cost budget (max_budget_usd), and wall-clock timeout in the loop
Add a system prompt instruction telling Claude to stop after 10 tool calls
Use tool_choice: none after 5 iterations to force Claude to stop using tools
Set a very high max_tokens value so Claude always has room to complete its response naturally

explanation: Production agentic loops require multiple safeguards: maximum iteration count (cap loop iterations), token budget tracking (monitor cumulative usage), cost budget (the Agent SDK supports max_budget_usd), and wall-clock timeouts. These are programmatic, deterministic controls that cannot be bypassed, unlike prompt-based instructions which are probabilistic.

why_others_wrong.b: System prompt instructions are probabilistic  --  Claude may follow them 'usually' but there is no guarantee. For production safety, you need deterministic programmatic controls, not prompt-based guidance.
why_others_wrong.c: Switching tool_choice mid-loop is technically possible but is a fragile approach. It forces a hard cutoff at an arbitrary point and may result in incomplete work. Proper loop guards allow the loop to exit gracefully.
why_others_wrong.d: High max_tokens only prevents per-response truncation. It does nothing to prevent the loop from running indefinitely across many iterations, each within the max_tokens limit.

doc_reference.quote: Maximum iteration count: Cap the number of loop iterations... Token budget: Track cumulative token usage... Cost budget: Set a maximum dollar spend (the Agent SDK supports max_budget_usd)... Timeout: Set wall-clock time limits

question: An e-commerce search agent uses server-executed web_search tools. During testing, some responses contain server_tool_use blocks but the developer's code tries to match them with tool_result blocks, causing errors. Which statement explains why this is incorrect?

options.text: Server-executed tools return results automatically in the same assistant turn  --  you should never construct a tool_result for them
Server-executed tools require an acknowledgment message instead of a tool_result
Server-executed tools require tool_result blocks but with a different format using the srvtoolu_ prefix
Server-executed tool results are delivered asynchronously via a separate callback endpoint

explanation: Server-executed tools (web_search, web_fetch, code_execution) run entirely on Anthropic's servers. Their results appear immediately after the server_tool_use block in the same assistant turn. By the time your application receives the response, execution is already complete. You must NEVER construct a tool_result for server-executed tools  --  only client-executed tools (with toolu_ prefix) require tool_result blocks.

why_others_wrong.b: There is no 'acknowledgment message' concept for server tools. The results are already present in the response when you receive it.
why_others_wrong.c: Server-executed tools do NOT use tool_result blocks at all, regardless of format or prefix. The server handles execution and results internally.
why_others_wrong.d: Server tool results are NOT delivered asynchronously. They appear in the same response, already complete, in the assistant turn immediately following the server_tool_use block.

doc_reference.quote: Anthropic runs the code  --  you never construct a tool_result. Result block appears immediately after server_tool_use block in same assistant turn. By the time you see them, execution is already complete.

question: Your CI/CD pipeline agent runs unattended and must handle all stop_reason values gracefully. When analyzing a response with stop_reason: 'refusal', what is the correct course of action?

options.text: Send the response back as a continuation request, similar to handling pause_turn
Switch to a different model that may not refuse the same request
Retry the request with a modified prompt that addresses Claude's concerns
Exit the loop, log the refusal, and handle it gracefully without retrying

explanation: When stop_reason is 'refusal', Claude has declined the request for safety reasons. The correct handling is to exit the loop, log the refusal for review, and handle it gracefully. Retrying the same or modified request is inappropriate because the refusal is a deliberate safety decision, not a transient error.

why_others_wrong.a: The continuation pattern (sending the response back) is specific to pause_turn, which indicates a server-side iteration limit. A refusal is a final decision, not a pause.
why_others_wrong.b: Switching models to avoid a safety refusal undermines the safety system. If Claude refuses a request, it's typically for a valid reason that should be respected.
why_others_wrong.c: Retrying with a modified prompt to circumvent a safety refusal is inappropriate. The refusal indicates Claude has determined the request should not be fulfilled for safety reasons.

doc_reference.quote: refusal: Claude refused to respond (safety). Exit loop; log and handle gracefully  --  do NOT retry.

question: You are building a data extraction agent that processes documents. The Agent SDK reports the loop ended with ResultMessage subtype 'error_max_turns'. What does this indicate and how should you handle it?

options.text: Claude's extended thinking exceeded the thinking budget  --  increase the budget_tokens parameter
The context window was exhausted  --  trigger compaction and restart the query from scratch
The agent hit the maxTurns limit set in query options  --  the task may be incomplete, so decide whether to resume or report partial results
Claude exceeded the maximum number of API requests allowed per minute  --  implement exponential backoff

explanation: In the Agent SDK, maxTurns counts tool-use turns only. When this limit is reached, the SDK returns a ResultMessage with subtype 'error_max_turns'. The result field will be absent (no final answer was produced). You should decide whether to resume the session (passing the session ID) to let the agent continue, or report partial results. This is a planned safeguard, not an unexpected error.

why_others_wrong.a: Thinking budget exhaustion would not produce this specific subtype. error_max_turns specifically relates to the agentic turn count reaching the configured limit.
why_others_wrong.b: Context exhaustion is a separate concern. The error_max_turns subtype specifically means the configured turn limit was reached, not that context was exhausted.
why_others_wrong.d: error_max_turns is not a rate limit issue. Rate limiting would be reported differently (as API errors, not as a ResultMessage subtype). maxTurns is a deliberate loop guard you configure.

doc_reference.quote: max_turns / maxTurns counts tool-use turns only... When hit, SDK returns ResultMessage with error_max_turns or error_max_budget_usd.

question: In the Agent SDK, the context window accumulates content across turns within a session. Which of the following is NOT automatically managed by the SDK to address growing context?

options.text: Automatic compaction when context approaches the limit, emitting a compact_boundary message
Using subagents for subtasks so intermediate work stays in the subagent's context
Prompt caching for repeated content like system prompts, tool definitions, and CLAUDE.md files
Automatic summarization of tool results that exceed a configurable size threshold

explanation: The SDK does NOT automatically summarize individual tool results. Everything accumulates in context: system prompt, tool definitions, conversation history, and tool I/O. The SDK manages context through automatic compaction (summarizing older history when approaching limits), prompt caching (for repeated content), and subagents (which isolate their work and return only summaries). But individual tool results are not automatically truncated or summarized.

why_others_wrong.a: Automatic compaction is a real SDK feature. When context approaches the limit, the SDK summarizes older history and emits a SystemMessage with subtype compact_boundary.
why_others_wrong.b: Using subagents is a real context management strategy. Subagents work in their own context window and return only a summary, keeping intermediate work out of the main context.
why_others_wrong.c: Prompt caching is automatic in the SDK. Repeated content like system prompts, tool definitions, and CLAUDE.md files are cached to reduce token costs.

doc_reference.quote: Everything accumulates: system prompt, tool definitions, conversation history, tool I/O. Repeated content (system prompt, tool defs, CLAUDE.md) is automatically prompt-cached.

question: You are reviewing an agentic loop implementation. The loop correctly handles tool_use, end_turn, and max_tokens stop reasons. During testing with server tools, the loop occasionally exits prematurely when it should continue processing. Which stop_reason is most likely being incorrectly handled?

options.text: end_turn  --  the loop sends an unnecessary continuation request instead of exiting
pause_turn  --  the loop exits instead of sending a continuation request
stop_sequence  --  the loop should continue when a stop sequence is matched
refusal  --  the loop treats it as continuable when it should exit

explanation: pause_turn occurs when the server-side tool loop hits its iteration limit (default 10). While it exits the standard tool-use loop (since it's not tool_use), it requires a continuation request  --  passing the paused response back to let Claude continue. If the loop simply exits on pause_turn without sending a continuation, the agent loses all the server-side work and appears to exit prematurely.

why_others_wrong.a: end_turn correctly signals that Claude has produced a final answer. The loop should exit on end_turn, so this would not explain premature exits during server tool processing.
why_others_wrong.c: stop_sequence correctly causes the loop to exit. The problem description specifically mentions server tools, and stop_sequence is unrelated to server tool iteration limits.
why_others_wrong.d: Refusal should indeed cause the loop to exit permanently. The described problem is premature exit during server tool processing, which points to pause_turn, not refusal.

doc_reference.quote: pause_turn: Server-side sampling loop reached iteration limit (default 10) while executing server tools. Continue by sending response back as-is in a subsequent request.

question: In a document processing pipeline, Claude requests multiple tools simultaneously. Your implementation sends each tool result in a separate user message. After deployment, you notice Claude stops requesting parallel tool calls. What is causing this regression?

options.text: Sending results in separate user messages trains the conversation pattern to avoid parallel calls  --  all results must be in a single user message
Parallel tool use requires tool_choice: any to be set, and the default auto mode doesn't support it
The tool definitions need disable_parallel_tool_use set to false explicitly to maintain parallelism
Claude has a built-in limit on parallel tool calls per session that resets after a timeout

explanation: When Claude requests multiple tools in parallel, all tool results must be sent in a single user message. Sending them in separate messages creates a conversation pattern that trains Claude away from parallel tool use in subsequent turns. The correct format is one user message containing all tool_result blocks.

why_others_wrong.b: Parallel tool use works with tool_choice: auto (the default). Claude can decide to use multiple tools simultaneously regardless of the tool_choice setting, as long as disable_parallel_tool_use is not set to true.
why_others_wrong.c: disable_parallel_tool_use defaults to false (parallel is enabled by default). You only need to set it to true if you want to DISABLE parallelism. The issue is the result message formatting, not the tool configuration.
why_others_wrong.d: There is no built-in per-session limit on parallel tool calls. The regression is caused by the conversation pattern created by separate result messages.

doc_reference.quote: All tool results must be sent in a single user message (NOT separate messages)... WRONG - reduces parallel tool use: [separate messages]. CORRECT - maintains parallel tool use: [single message]

question: An automated testing agent's agentic loop runs well in development but crashes in production with 400 errors after certain tool executions. Analyzing the request payloads, you find the developer added helpful context messages between tool use and tool result messages. Why does this cause errors?

options.text: The API requires that tool_result blocks immediately follow their corresponding tool_use blocks with no intermediate messages allowed
Context messages exceed the maximum content block limit per message, triggering a size validation error
Text content blocks are not allowed in messages that also contain tool_result blocks
The additional messages cause the conversation history to exceed the context window, resulting in a 400 error

explanation: This is a critical ordering rule in the tool use contract: you CANNOT include any messages between the assistant's tool_use message and the user's tool_result message. The tool result must immediately follow the tool use in message history. Violating this produces a hard 400 API error, not a degraded response.

why_others_wrong.b: The error is not about content block limits or size. It's specifically about message ordering  --  intermediate messages between tool_use and tool_result are not allowed.
why_others_wrong.c: Text content blocks ARE allowed in the same message as tool_result blocks  --  but the text must come AFTER all tool_result blocks in the content array. The issue here is intermediate messages between turns, not content ordering within a message.
why_others_wrong.d: Context window overflow returns a different error or stop_reason (model_context_window_exceeded), not a 400 error. The 400 error is specifically caused by violating the message ordering constraint.

doc_reference.quote: Tool result blocks must immediately follow their corresponding tool use blocks in message history. You CANNOT include any messages between the assistant's tool use message and the user's tool result message.

question: A healthcare data agent uses the Agent SDK with a 30-turn limit and $5 budget. The SDK returns a ResultMessage. Which fields should you always check regardless of whether the result subtype is 'success' or an error variant?

options.text: Only the stop_reason field, since all other fields are undefined on error subtypes
The structured_output field, which contains the final answer regardless of completion status
The total_cost_usd, usage, num_turns, and session_id fields, as these are present on all subtypes
Only the result text field, since cost data is only available on successful completions

explanation: All ResultMessage subtypes carry total_cost_usd, usage, num_turns, and session_id. The result field is only present on success subtypes. Both success and error results include usage and cost data, so you should always read cost data regardless of the subtype to ensure proper cost tracking.

why_others_wrong.a: Multiple fields beyond stop_reason are available on error subtypes, including total_cost_usd, usage, num_turns, and session_id. These are essential for monitoring and debugging.
why_others_wrong.b: The structured_output field is only available on successful completions with structured output configured. It is not present on error subtypes.
why_others_wrong.d: Cost data is available on ALL result subtypes, not just successful ones. Always read cost data regardless of subtype to ensure proper cost tracking.

doc_reference.quote: All subtypes carry total_cost_usd, usage, num_turns, session_id... Both success and error results include usage and total_cost_usd. Always read cost data regardless of subtype.

question: A compliance audit agent must run with strict cost controls. When configuring the Agent SDK, what is the relationship between maxTurns and maxBudgetUsd?

options.text: Both are independent limits with no default  --  when either is hit, the SDK returns a ResultMessage with the corresponding error subtype
maxBudgetUsd automatically calculates and sets maxTurns based on the average cost per turn
They are mutually exclusive  --  you can set one or the other but not both
maxTurns sets a hard limit on API calls while maxBudgetUsd sets a soft advisory budget that may be exceeded

explanation: maxTurns and maxBudgetUsd are independent options with no default limit (unlimited by default). They can be used separately or together. When maxTurns is hit, the SDK returns ResultMessage with subtype error_max_turns. When maxBudgetUsd is hit, it returns error_max_budget_usd. Neither depends on the other.

why_others_wrong.b: There is no automatic calculation between the two. They operate independently on different metrics (turn count vs dollar cost).
why_others_wrong.c: They are not mutually exclusive. You can set both simultaneously, and whichever limit is reached first will trigger the corresponding error subtype.
why_others_wrong.d: Both are hard limits in the SDK context. When either is hit, the loop stops and returns an error ResultMessage. The Agent SDK maxBudgetUsd is not advisory  --  it terminates the loop.

doc_reference.quote: max_turns / maxTurns: No limit (default). max_budget_usd / maxBudgetUsd: No limit (default). When hit, SDK returns ResultMessage with error_max_turns or error_max_budget_usd.

question: You are building an API integration agent. After Claude calls a tool, your code returns a tool_result with a descriptive error message but forgets to set is_error: true. What is the practical consequence?

options.text: The API will reject the tool_result with a 400 error because error content requires the is_error flag
Claude will automatically detect the error from the content and behave identically to when is_error is set
Claude will treat the error text as a successful result and may use it incorrectly in subsequent reasoning
The tool_result will be silently discarded and Claude will retry the tool call automatically

explanation: Without is_error: true, Claude treats the returned content as a successful tool result. If your content says 'ConnectionError: service unavailable' but is_error is not set, Claude may incorporate that text as if it were valid data  --  for example, telling the user the connection error IS the answer. The is_error flag explicitly signals to Claude that the execution failed, enabling it to reason about the failure and adapt its strategy.

why_others_wrong.a: The API does not require is_error for error content. The is_error field is optional, and the API will accept the tool_result regardless. The issue is semantic, not syntactic.
why_others_wrong.b: While Claude may sometimes infer that content looks like an error, it is not guaranteed. Without the explicit is_error signal, Claude may treat error text as valid data. The is_error flag provides a clear, unambiguous signal.
why_others_wrong.d: Tool results are never silently discarded. The content is always presented to Claude. Without is_error, Claude receives the content as a successful result and continues reasoning with it.

doc_reference.quote: Set to true if tool execution resulted in an error. Claude will incorporate the error into its response. Best practice: Write instructive error messages including what went wrong and what to try next.

question: Your data migration agent sends a user message that contains a text block followed by a tool_result block. The API returns a 400 error. What ordering rule was violated?

options.text: Text blocks in user messages are limited to 100 characters when accompanied by tool_result blocks
All tool_result blocks must appear before any text content in the content array of a user message
User messages cannot contain both text and tool_result blocks in the same message
Tool_result blocks can only appear in dedicated user messages with no other content types

explanation: The API enforces a strict ordering rule: in a user message containing tool results, all tool_result blocks must come FIRST in the content array, before any text blocks. Placing text before tool_result causes a 400 error. This is not a soft guideline  --  it's a hard API constraint.

why_others_wrong.a: There is no character limit on text blocks within messages containing tool results. The issue is purely about ordering, not size.
why_others_wrong.c: User messages CAN contain both text and tool_result blocks. The constraint is on ordering: tool_result blocks must come first, then text blocks.
why_others_wrong.d: Tool_result blocks can coexist with text blocks in the same message. The rule is that tool_result blocks must appear before text blocks in the content array.

doc_reference.quote: In the user message containing tool results, tool_result blocks must come FIRST in the content array. Any text must come AFTER all tool results... WRONG - causes 400 error: text before tool_result.

question: You want to guarantee that Claude always calls your extract_invoice_data tool on every request, without any preamble text. Which configuration achieves this?

options.text: Set tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_invoice_data"}  --  Claude must use that specific tool with no natural language before it
Set tool_choice to 'any' and provide only the extract_invoice_data tool  --  Claude must use it but may add preamble text
Set tool_choice to 'auto' and add 'Always use extract_invoice_data' to the system prompt
Set tool_choice to 'none' and handle the tool call in a PreToolUse hook instead

explanation: Setting tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_invoice_data"} forces Claude to use that specific tool. When tool_choice is 'any' or 'tool', the API prefills the assistant message to force tool use, meaning Claude will NOT emit natural language before tool_use blocks. This guarantees both the specific tool call and the absence of preamble text.

why_others_wrong.b: While tool_choice: any forces Claude to use a tool, the key detail is wrong: when tool_choice is any or tool, Claude does NOT add preamble text. The API prefills the assistant message, preventing natural language before tool_use blocks.
why_others_wrong.c: tool_choice: auto lets Claude decide whether to use tools. Even with system prompt instructions, Claude may choose not to call the tool or may add natural language before doing so. System prompts are probabilistic, not guaranteed.
why_others_wrong.d: tool_choice: none prevents Claude from using any tools. Hooks intercept tool calls, but with tool_choice: none, there would be no tool call to intercept.

doc_reference.quote: When tool_choice is any or tool, the API prefills the assistant message to force tool use. This means the model will NOT emit natural language before tool_use blocks.

question: A developer enables extended thinking on their research assistant and also sets tool_choice to 'any' to force tool use. The API returns an error. What is the root cause?

options.text: Extended thinking requires a minimum of 3 tools to be defined before it can be used
Extended thinking only works with server-executed tools, not client-defined tools
Extended thinking only supports tool_choice: auto and tool_choice: none  --  using 'any' or 'tool' causes an error
Extended thinking is not compatible with any tool use at all

explanation: Extended thinking has a compatibility constraint: it only supports tool_choice: auto and tool_choice: none. Setting tool_choice to 'any' or 'tool' with extended thinking enabled produces an API error. This is because the prefill mechanism used by any/tool conflicts with extended thinking's operation.

why_others_wrong.a: There is no minimum tool count requirement for extended thinking. The restriction is specifically on the tool_choice parameter value, not the number of tools.
why_others_wrong.b: Extended thinking works with all tool types. The restriction is on the tool_choice parameter (only auto and none), not on the category of tools being used.
why_others_wrong.d: Extended thinking IS compatible with tool use  --  but only when tool_choice is set to auto. Claude can still choose to use tools; it just can't be forced to use them with any or tool.

doc_reference.quote: Extended thinking only supports auto and none (not any or tool).

question: You are integrating a compliance-checking tool that must receive inputs matching an exact JSON Schema  --  no extra fields, no type mismatches. Your team also needs to ensure Claude always calls this tool. Which combination achieves both guarantees?

options.text: Set strict: true on the tool definition and tool_choice: {"type": "any"} to guarantee both schema compliance and tool invocation
Set strict: true on the tool definition and tool_choice: auto to let Claude decide when compliance checks are needed
Set strict: true and disable_parallel_tool_use: true to force exactly one schema-validated call
Use a PreToolUse hook to validate inputs and tool_choice: any to guarantee invocation

explanation: strict: true uses grammar-constrained sampling to guarantee tool inputs match the JSON Schema exactly. tool_choice: any guarantees Claude will use one of the provided tools. Combining them gives you both guaranteed tool invocation AND guaranteed schema compliance. The documentation specifically recommends this combination.

why_others_wrong.b: strict: true guarantees schema compliance, but tool_choice: auto means Claude may choose NOT to call the tool at all. For a compliance-checking tool that must always run, you need guaranteed invocation.
why_others_wrong.c: disable_parallel_tool_use limits to exactly one tool call when combined with any/tool, but it doesn't help with schema validation. While it adds a constraint, it doesn't combine the two specific guarantees needed: schema compliance + tool invocation.
why_others_wrong.d: A PreToolUse hook can validate inputs, but it runs after Claude generates the call  --  meaning invalid inputs would be blocked but the turn would be wasted. strict: true prevents invalid inputs at the sampling level, which is more efficient. Also, hooks validate semantics while strict validates structure.

doc_reference.quote: Combine with tool_choice: {"type": "any"} for guaranteed tool call AND schema compliance.

question: Your team is building a tool for processing medical records with strict: true enabled. A security reviewer raises a concern about Protected Health Information (PHI) in the tool definition. What is the specific risk?

options.text: PHI in tool schemas would be visible in API error messages sent to the client
strict: true sends tool schemas to a third-party validation service, exposing PHI to external systems
PHI in tool names violates the tool name regex pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$ and would cause compilation errors
Compiled schemas with strict: true are cached separately from message content for up to 24 hours  --  PHI in schemas could persist in this separate cache

explanation: When strict: true is enabled, compiled schemas are cached for up to 24 hours since last use. Critically, these schemas are cached separately from message content. This means PHI included in tool schema definitions could persist in this separate cache outside normal data retention controls. The documentation explicitly warns: 'PHI must NOT be in tool schema definitions (cached separately from message content).'

why_others_wrong.a: The concern is not about error messages but about the schema caching mechanism. Compiled schemas are stored separately from message content with different retention characteristics.
why_others_wrong.b: strict: true uses grammar-constrained sampling within Anthropic's infrastructure. It does not send schemas to third-party services.
why_others_wrong.c: PHI could appear in description fields, enum values, or property names within the input_schema  --  not necessarily in the tool name itself. The regex constraint applies to tool names, not to the entire schema definition.

doc_reference.quote: Compiled schemas cached up to 24 hours since last use. PHI must NOT be in tool schema definitions (cached separately from message content).

question: You are building an enterprise knowledge base agent with 500 tools. Adding all tool definitions to the system prompt consumes a large portion of the context window. Which tool configuration property preserves prompt cache while enabling on-demand tool discovery?

options.text: cache_control on each tool definition to set breakpoints for the prompt cache
defer_loading: true which excludes tools from the system prompt and loads them via tool search on demand
allowed_callers: ['code_execution_20260120'] to move tools out of the direct calling path
eager_input_streaming: true to reduce the token overhead of tool definitions

explanation: Tools with defer_loading: true are stripped from the rendered tools section before the cache key is computed. They don't appear in the system-prompt prefix at all, which preserves existing prompt cache entries when you add new deferred tools. Tools are then discovered on demand via tool search (3-5 most relevant tools loaded per search). This is specifically designed for scenarios with many tools.

why_others_wrong.a: cache_control sets breakpoints within the prompt cache but doesn't remove tools from the system prompt. With 500 tools, all definitions still consume context regardless of cache breakpoints.
why_others_wrong.c: allowed_callers restricts who can invoke a tool (direct model calls vs code execution sandbox). It doesn't remove tools from the system prompt or reduce context consumption.
why_others_wrong.d: eager_input_streaming controls how tool input is streamed during generation. It doesn't reduce the token overhead of tool definitions in the system prompt.

doc_reference.quote: Tools with defer_loading: true are stripped from rendered tools section before cache key computed. Don't appear in system-prompt prefix at all. Preserves prompt cache  --  adding deferred tools won't invalidate existing cache entries.

question: You want a custom tool that can ONLY be invoked from within Claude's code execution sandbox, not directly by the model. How do you configure allowed_callers to achieve this?

options.text: Set allowed_callers to ['direct'] to restrict it to direct model invocations only
Set allowed_callers to ['code_execution_20260120'] without including 'direct'  --  omitting 'direct' blocks model access
Omit allowed_callers entirely, which defaults to sandbox-only execution
Set allowed_callers to [] (empty array) to disable all callers, then enable sandbox access via a hook

explanation: The allowed_callers property controls which callers can invoke a tool. Setting it to ['code_execution_20260120'] means only code running inside the code execution sandbox can call this tool. The critical detail is that omitting 'direct' from the array means the model CANNOT call it directly in a tool_use block. The default when allowed_callers is omitted is ['direct'], which allows model calls.

why_others_wrong.a: Setting allowed_callers to ['direct'] allows only direct model invocations  --  the opposite of what we want. We need sandbox-only access.
why_others_wrong.c: Omitting allowed_callers entirely defaults to 'direct' (model can call it directly). This is the opposite of sandbox-only  --  the tool would be accessible to the model directly.
why_others_wrong.d: An empty array would block all callers including the sandbox. Hooks run in the application process and cannot grant sandbox calling permissions after the fact.

doc_reference.quote: allowed_callers values: 'direct'  --  Model can call this tool directly (DEFAULT if omitted). 'code_execution_20260120'  --  Code running inside sandbox can call this tool. Omitting 'direct' means tool is callable ONLY from within code execution.

question: You define 10 tools for a chat assistant and notice that even before any tool is called, the first API request costs 346 tokens just for tool overhead. A colleague suggests adding input_examples to your server-executed web_search tool. Why will this fail?

options.text: input_examples are only supported on user-defined and Anthropic-schema client tools, NOT on server-executed tools
Server tools already have built-in examples and reject additional ones to prevent conflicts
input_examples require strict: true to be enabled first, and web_search does not support strict mode
input_examples only work with tool_choice: tool and web_search always uses auto

explanation: The input_examples property is available on user-defined and Anthropic-schema client tools only  --  NOT on server-executed tools. Server tools (web_search, web_fetch, code_execution) do not accept input_examples. Each example must validate against the input_schema, and they add approximately 20-50 tokens for simple examples or 100-200 for complex ones.

why_others_wrong.b: Server tools do not have a built-in examples mechanism that would conflict. The limitation is simply that input_examples is not supported for server-side tools by design.
why_others_wrong.c: input_examples and strict: true are independent features. You don't need strict mode to use input_examples. The limitation is specifically that server tools don't support input_examples at all.
why_others_wrong.d: input_examples work with any tool_choice setting. The limitation has nothing to do with tool_choice  --  it's about the tool execution category (server vs client).

doc_reference.quote: input_examples: Array of example input objects. Available On: User-defined and Anthropic-schema client tools only (NOT server tools).

question: You are evaluating which tool category to use for a bash command execution feature. Anthropic provides bash_20250124 as a client-executed tool. What advantage does using this Anthropic-schema tool have over creating your own custom 'run_bash' tool with an identical schema?

options.text: Anthropic-schema tools have lower token overhead because their definitions are not included in the system prompt
Anthropic-schema tools have schemas that are trained-in  --  Claude has been optimized on thousands of successful trajectories using these exact schemas, so it calls them more reliably
Anthropic-schema tools automatically validate inputs using grammar-constrained sampling without needing strict: true
Anthropic-schema tools execute faster because they run on Anthropic's servers

explanation: Anthropic-schema client tools like bash_20250124 are 'trained-in'  --  Claude has been specifically optimized on thousands of successful trajectories using these exact tool schemas. This means Claude calls them more reliably than custom tools with similar functionality. Despite having the same execution model as user-defined tools (client-side execution with tool_use/tool_result), the trained-in schemas give Claude better understanding of how to use them.

why_others_wrong.a: Anthropic-schema tools ARE included in the system prompt and consume tokens like any other tool definition. They don't have special token treatment.
why_others_wrong.c: Grammar-constrained sampling (strict: true) is a separate feature that must be explicitly enabled. Anthropic-schema tools do not automatically use it.
why_others_wrong.d: Anthropic-schema tools are CLIENT-executed, not server-executed. Your application handles the execution, just like user-defined tools. The execution location is identical.

doc_reference.quote: These schemas are trained-in  --  Claude has been optimized on thousands of successful trajectories using these exact signatures, so calls them more reliably.

question: You configure disable_parallel_tool_use: true with tool_choice: 'any'. How many tool calls will Claude make per response?

options.text: One or more  --  disable_parallel_tool_use has no effect when tool_choice is 'any'
Zero or one  --  Claude may choose not to call any tools
At most one  --  Claude will make zero or one tool call
Exactly one  --  Claude must use a tool and is limited to one call

explanation: When disable_parallel_tool_use: true is combined with tool_choice: any, Claude must use exactly one tool. The 'any' setting forces at least one tool call, and disable_parallel_tool_use: true limits it to at most one. Together they constrain to exactly one. With tool_choice: auto, the same setting would give 'at most one' (zero or one).

why_others_wrong.a: disable_parallel_tool_use absolutely has an effect with tool_choice: any. Without it, 'any' allows one or more tool calls. With it, 'any' is constrained to exactly one.
why_others_wrong.b: tool_choice: any means Claude MUST use one of the provided tools  --  it cannot choose zero. Combined with disable_parallel_tool_use: true, the result is exactly one.
why_others_wrong.c: 'At most one' would be the behavior with tool_choice: auto + disable_parallel_tool_use: true, where Claude might choose not to call any tool. With 'any', at least one call is guaranteed.

doc_reference.quote: disable_parallel_tool_use=true + tool_choice: auto = at most one tool. disable_parallel_tool_use=true + tool_choice: any/tool = exactly one tool.

question: Your organization requires Zero Data Retention (ZDR) compliance. You want to use the latest web_search_20260209 tool. What must you do to make it ZDR-eligible?

options.text: Use the older web_search_20250305 version instead  --  the 20260209 version cannot be made ZDR-eligible under any configuration
Add a ZDR: true flag to the tool configuration alongside the beta header
Set allowed_callers to ['direct'] to disable dynamic filtering, which uses code execution internally
Enable strict: true on the web_search tool definition

explanation: The web_search_20260209 version is NOT ZDR-eligible by default because it uses code execution internally for dynamic content filtering. To use it with ZDR, set allowed_callers to ['direct'] to disable the dynamic filtering feature. This prevents the internal code execution that breaks ZDR eligibility. Alternatively, you can use the basic web_search_20250305 version which is ZDR-eligible by default.

why_others_wrong.a: While using the older version is an alternative, the statement that 20260209 'cannot be made ZDR-eligible' is false. Setting allowed_callers to ['direct'] disables dynamic filtering and makes it ZDR-eligible.
why_others_wrong.b: There is no 'ZDR: true' flag on tool configurations. ZDR eligibility is determined by the tool version and its internal features, managed through allowed_callers configuration.
why_others_wrong.d: strict: true affects tool input schema validation, not ZDR eligibility. The ZDR issue is specifically about the dynamic filtering feature that uses code execution internally.

doc_reference.quote: _20260209 versions are NOT ZDR-eligible by default (use code execution internally). To use _20260209 with ZDR: set allowed_callers: ['direct'] to disable dynamic filtering.

question: You are designing a customer service system that classifies incoming tickets as general inquiries, refund requests, or technical issues, then routes each to a specialized handler with its own optimized prompt. Which workflow pattern best describes this architecture?

options.text: Routing  --  input is classified and directed to specialized downstream tasks with task-specific prompt optimization
Prompt chaining  --  each classification step feeds into the next in sequence
Parallelization with voting  --  the same ticket is evaluated by multiple classifiers
Orchestrator-workers  --  a central LLM dynamically decomposes the task into subtasks

explanation: This is the Routing pattern: classify input and direct it to specialized downstream tasks. The key benefit is separation of concerns  --  each route (general, refund, technical) can have its own optimized prompt. Anthropic's example use cases include exactly this: 'Customer service triage (general/refunds/technical)' and 'Model selection routing (simple → Haiku, complex → Sonnet)'.

why_others_wrong.b: Prompt chaining involves sequential LLM calls where each processes the previous output with programmatic gates. The triage system routes to independent handlers, not a sequential chain.
why_others_wrong.c: Parallelization with voting runs the same task multiple times for diverse outputs. The ticket system routes to different specialized handlers, not multiple instances of the same classifier.
why_others_wrong.d: Orchestrator-workers involves dynamic task decomposition by a central LLM. The ticket routing system uses a predefined classification scheme, not dynamic decomposition. The routes are known in advance.

doc_reference.quote: Routing: Classifies input and directs to specialized downstream tasks. Benefit: Separation of concerns enabling task-specific prompt optimization. Use cases: Customer service triage (general/refunds/technical).

question: A content generation pipeline first creates an outline, then validates it against guidelines, then expands each section into full text. Each step uses the output of the previous step, and programmatic 'gates' between steps verify the output is on track. Which workflow pattern is this?

options.text: Orchestrator-workers  --  the pipeline dynamically decides the steps
Parallelization with sectioning  --  independent subtasks run simultaneously
Prompt chaining  --  sequential LLM calls with programmatic gates verifying intermediate steps
Evaluator-optimizer  --  the validation step provides iterative feedback

explanation: This is prompt chaining: sequential LLM calls where each step processes the previous output, with programmatic 'gates' verifying intermediate steps remain on track. The key features are: sequential processing, output flowing from one step to the next, and gates that verify quality between steps. Anthropic's example: 'Document outline → validation → full document'.

why_others_wrong.a: Orchestrator-workers involve dynamic task decomposition. The content pipeline has predetermined steps (outline → validate → expand), not dynamic decomposition.
why_others_wrong.b: Parallelization runs subtasks simultaneously. This pipeline is explicitly sequential  --  each step depends on the previous step's output.
why_others_wrong.d: Evaluator-optimizer is an iterative loop where one LLM generates and another evaluates with feedback, refining the same output repeatedly. This pipeline is a forward-flowing sequence, not an iterative refinement loop.

doc_reference.quote: Prompt Chaining: Sequential LLM calls where each processes previous output. Key feature: Programmatic 'gates' verify intermediate steps remain on track. Use cases: Document outline → validation → full document.

question: You need a system where a central LLM analyzes a refactoring request, dynamically decides which files need modification based on the specific input, and delegates each file change to a worker LLM. A colleague suggests this is the parallelization pattern. Why is this classification incorrect?

options.text: Parallelization cannot delegate to separate LLM instances, only to the same instance
Parallelization only works with identical prompts (voting), not different tasks for each worker
In parallelization, subtasks are predefined in your code. In this system, the central LLM dynamically determines the decomposition  --  this is the orchestrator-workers (hub-and-spoke) pattern
Parallelization requires at least 5 simultaneous workers, which this system may not use

explanation: The key distinction between parallelization and orchestrator-workers is WHO determines the subtasks. In parallelization, subtasks are predefined in your code (you know in advance what to parallelize). In orchestrator-workers (hub-and-spoke), the orchestrator LLM dynamically determines the decomposition based on specific input analysis. Since the central LLM decides which files need changes based on the refactoring request, this is orchestrator-workers.

why_others_wrong.a: Parallelization can and does delegate to separate LLM instances. The distinction is about how subtasks are determined, not about how many instances are used.
why_others_wrong.b: Parallelization has two variations: sectioning (different independent subtasks) and voting (same task multiple times). Sectioning does support different tasks. The issue is that in parallelization, those different tasks are predefined, not dynamically determined.
why_others_wrong.d: There is no minimum worker count for parallelization. The distinguishing factor is whether subtask decomposition is predefined (parallelization) or dynamic (orchestrator-workers).

doc_reference.quote: Key distinction from parallelization: Orchestrator determines decomposition based on specific input analysis (NOT predefined subtasks). Use cases: Multi-file code modifications.

question: A literary translation system uses one LLM to translate text and another LLM to evaluate the translation quality, providing specific feedback. The translator then revises based on the feedback, and this cycle repeats until quality criteria are met. Which workflow pattern is this?

options.text: Prompt chaining with two sequential steps
Parallelization with voting using multiple translation attempts
Routing  --  the evaluator routes feedback to the appropriate translation model
Evaluator-optimizer  --  one LLM generates while another evaluates with feedback in an iterative loop

explanation: This is the evaluator-optimizer pattern: an iterative loop where one LLM generates (the translator) and another evaluates with feedback (the quality assessor). The cycle repeats until criteria are met. Anthropic specifically lists 'literary translation through iterative refinement' as a use case for this pattern. Success indicators include clear evaluation criteria and LLM ability to articulate useful critiques.

why_others_wrong.a: Prompt chaining is sequential with forward flow. The key difference here is the iterative feedback loop  --  the output goes back to the generator for revision, rather than flowing forward to a new step.
why_others_wrong.b: Parallelization with voting runs the same task multiple times independently. This system has an iterative feedback loop between generator and evaluator, not independent parallel attempts.
why_others_wrong.c: Routing classifies input and directs to downstream handlers. The evaluator here provides feedback that loops back to the same generator, not routing to different handlers.

doc_reference.quote: Evaluator-Optimizer: Iterative loop  --  one LLM generates, another evaluates with feedback. Use cases: Literary translation through iterative refinement.

question: A code security review system runs two independent reviewers in parallel on the same PR  --  one checks for SQL injection vulnerabilities and the other checks for XSS. Results are aggregated programmatically. A third configuration runs the SQL injection check three times with different prompts and takes a majority vote. Which parallelization variations are being used?

options.text: Neither is parallelization  --  this is the orchestrator-workers pattern since the system dynamically decides what to review
The independent reviewers use sectioning (different subtasks in parallel); the triple SQL check uses voting (same task multiple times)
The independent reviewers use voting; the triple SQL check uses sectioning
Both use sectioning  --  they all run different prompts on different aspects

explanation: Parallelization has two distinct variations. Sectioning runs independent subtasks in parallel (the SQL injection and XSS reviewers doing different checks simultaneously). Voting runs the same task multiple times for diverse outputs (the SQL injection check running three times with different prompts, taking a majority vote). The reviews are predefined, not dynamically determined by an LLM.

why_others_wrong.a: This IS parallelization because the subtasks are predefined in the code (the system knows in advance it will run SQL and XSS checks). In orchestrator-workers, the central LLM would dynamically decide what to review based on the input.
why_others_wrong.c: This reverses the variations. The independent reviewers doing different checks are sectioning (different independent subtasks). The triple SQL check on the same task is voting.
why_others_wrong.d: The triple SQL check is not sectioning because all three instances check the same thing (SQL injection) with different prompts. Sectioning involves different independent subtasks, not the same task repeated.

doc_reference.quote: Two variations: Sectioning  --  Independent subtasks run in parallel. Voting  --  Same task executed multiple times for diverse outputs.

question: During an architecture review, a team proposes building a complex multi-agent system with 5 different specialized models, custom routing logic, and a sophisticated state management layer. Based on Anthropic's three core principles for agent success, what concern should you raise?

options.text: The design needs more agents  --  Anthropic recommends at least 7 specialized agents for production systems
The design violates the Simplicity principle  --  Anthropic advises minimizing agent design complexity and starting with simple LLM calls
The design needs a framework to manage the complexity  --  Anthropic recommends always using agent frameworks
The design should use a single model throughout  --  multi-model architectures are not supported by the Claude API

explanation: Anthropic's three core principles for agent success are Simplicity, Transparency, and Agent-Computer Interface (ACI). The Simplicity principle advises minimizing agent design complexity. Anthropic's key guidance is to 'start with simple LLM calls optimized with retrieval and contextual examples' before adding complexity. A system with 5 models, custom routing, and sophisticated state management likely violates this principle.

why_others_wrong.a: Anthropic does not recommend any minimum number of agents. In fact, the opposite  --  the Simplicity principle suggests fewer moving parts, not more.
why_others_wrong.c: Anthropic explicitly warns that 'frameworks simplify initial implementation but create abstraction layers obscuring prompts and responses' and recommends starting with direct LLM API calls.
why_others_wrong.d: Multi-model architectures are fully supported. The concern is not about technical capability but about unnecessary complexity.

doc_reference.quote: Three Core Principles: 1. Simplicity: Minimize agent design complexity. Key guidance: Start with simple LLM calls optimized with retrieval and contextual examples.

question: After SWE-bench evaluations, Anthropic noted they 'spent more time optimizing tools than the overall prompt.' They gave an example of requiring absolute filepaths after the model kept making errors with relative paths. What principle does this exemplify?

options.text: Transparency  --  making the model's planning steps visible to users
Simplicity  --  reducing the number of tools available to the model
Routing  --  directing filepath-related requests to a specialized file handling model
The poka-yoke concept within the Agent-Computer Interface (ACI) principle  --  designing tools to make errors harder

explanation: This exemplifies the poka-yoke concept (error-proofing) within the Agent-Computer Interface (ACI) principle. ACI is the agent analog of UX  --  it involves thoroughly documenting and testing tools, and implementing modifications that make errors harder. Requiring absolute filepaths is a classic poka-yoke: it eliminates an entire class of errors (relative path resolution issues) by design.

why_others_wrong.a: Transparency is about explicitly displaying planning steps to users for verification. Requiring absolute filepaths is about tool design, not about making plans visible.
why_others_wrong.b: Simplicity is about minimizing design complexity. Requiring absolute filepaths doesn't reduce tool count  --  it improves tool design to prevent errors.
why_others_wrong.c: This is a tool design modification, not a routing pattern. The filepath requirement is baked into the tool's interface, not handled by routing to specialized models.

doc_reference.quote: Implement 'poka-yoke' modifications making errors harder. Example: Absolute filepath requirements replaced relative paths after model errors. ACI: Thoroughly document and test tools.

question: A developer is parsing Claude's text output with regex to determine whether to execute a database query. Anthropic's documentation offers a clear heuristic for this situation. What does it recommend?

options.text: Use stop_sequences to detect the decision point instead of regex
Implement a separate classifier model to interpret Claude's text output
If you're writing a regex to extract a decision from model output, that decision should have been a tool call
Use a more sophisticated parser instead of regex for better reliability

explanation: Anthropic's heuristic is explicit: 'If you're writing a regex to extract a decision from model output, that decision should have been a tool call.' This means if you need a structured decision from Claude, define a tool that Claude calls with the structured decision as its input. This gives you guaranteed JSON structure instead of fragile text parsing.

why_others_wrong.a: Stop sequences can mark the end of generation but don't extract structured decisions from text. The recommendation is to use tool calls for structured outputs.
why_others_wrong.b: Adding a classifier model adds complexity. The simpler solution is to have Claude express the decision directly through a tool call with structured parameters.
why_others_wrong.d: The recommendation is not about parser sophistication. Even a perfect parser is the wrong approach  --  the decision should be expressed through tool use, not text parsing.

doc_reference.quote: Key heuristic: 'If you're writing a regex to extract a decision from model output, that decision should have been a tool call.'

question: Anthropic describes autonomous agents as 'LLMs maintaining control over task execution through environmental feedback loops.' Which of the following is NOT listed as a key requirement for autonomous agents?

options.text: Pause capability at checkpoints or when encountering blockers
Stopping conditions such as maximum iterations to maintain operational control
Gaining ground truth from the environment at each step through tool results and code execution feedback
Pre-defined decision trees mapping every possible state to a specific action

explanation: Pre-defined decision trees would make this a workflow, not an autonomous agent. The distinction between workflows and agents is that workflows use predefined code paths while agents dynamically direct their own processes. The three key requirements for autonomous agents are: gaining ground truth from the environment, pause capability at checkpoints, and stopping conditions (maximum iterations).

why_others_wrong.a: Pause capability is explicitly listed as a key requirement. Agents need the ability to pause at checkpoints or when encountering blockers.
why_others_wrong.b: Stopping conditions are explicitly listed as a key requirement. Maximum iterations and other stopping conditions maintain operational control and prevent runaway execution.
why_others_wrong.c: Gaining ground truth from the environment is explicitly listed as a key requirement. Tool results and code execution feedback give agents the environmental feedback they need to make decisions.

doc_reference.quote: Key requirements: Gain ground truth from environment at each step... Pause capability at checkpoints... Stopping conditions (maximum iterations) to maintain operational control.

question: You need to evaluate whether a piece of user content is appropriate. The system should run the same content through 5 different prompts and accept the content only if 4 out of 5 approve. Which specific parallelization variation implements this?

options.text: Voting  --  the same task executed multiple times with diverse outputs aggregated by configurable vote threshold
Orchestrator-workers  --  a central LLM dynamically decides how many checks to run
Evaluator-optimizer  --  one model evaluates and another optimizes the content until it passes
Sectioning  --  each prompt handles an independent aspect of appropriateness

explanation: This is the Voting variation of parallelization: the same task (content appropriateness check) is executed multiple times (5 prompts) with diverse outputs, aggregated using a configurable vote threshold (4 out of 5 must approve). Anthropic specifically lists 'Content appropriateness with configurable vote thresholds' as a use case for voting.

why_others_wrong.b: The number and nature of checks are predefined (5 checks with a 4/5 threshold), not dynamically decided by an LLM. This is parallelization, not orchestrator-workers.
why_others_wrong.c: Evaluator-optimizer is an iterative refinement loop. This system runs parallel checks with majority voting  --  there is no iterative feedback loop.
why_others_wrong.d: Sectioning runs different independent subtasks in parallel. All 5 instances here are doing the same task (appropriateness check), not different tasks.

doc_reference.quote: Voting: Same task executed multiple times for diverse outputs. Use cases: Content appropriateness with configurable vote thresholds.

question: You need to decide between using a workflow or an autonomous agent for a new system. According to Anthropic's guidance, which combination of requirements most strongly indicates you should use an autonomous agent rather than a workflow?

options.text: High-volume batch processing with identical tasks and tight latency requirements
Well-defined steps, consistent output format, and predictable execution time
A fixed sequence of 3 transformations with programmatic validation between each step
Open-ended problems with unpredictable step counts, requiring model-driven decision-making in trusted environments

explanation: Anthropic recommends agents for: open-ended problems with unpredictable step counts, tasks where fixed paths cannot be hardcoded, and trusted environments requiring autonomous scaling. These scenarios need 'flexibility and model-driven decision-making.' In contrast, workflows are for 'predictability and consistency for well-defined tasks.'

why_others_wrong.a: High-volume batch processing with identical tasks is best served by workflows (specifically parallelization/sectioning). Agents are for tasks requiring dynamic decision-making, not repetitive identical tasks.
why_others_wrong.b: Well-defined steps, consistent output, and predictable timing are hallmarks of workflows, not agents. These benefit from predefined code paths.
why_others_wrong.c: A fixed sequence with 3 transformations and programmatic validation is exactly the prompt chaining workflow pattern. The predetermined steps and gates make this a clear workflow, not an agent.

doc_reference.quote: When to use agents: Open-ended problems with unpredictable step counts; Tasks where fixed paths cannot be hardcoded; Trusted environments requiring autonomous scaling.

question: You are designing a multi-agent system in Claude Code where the main conversation coordinates three specialized workers: a code analyzer, a documentation writer, and a test generator. The code analyzer's results feed into the documentation writer. Which orchestration pattern does this represent in Claude Code's architecture?

options.text: Agent teams  --  the three workers communicate directly with each other
Parallelization pattern  --  all three subagents run simultaneously and results are aggregated
Nested subagents  --  the code analyzer spawns the documentation writer as a child subagent
Coordinator-subagent pattern  --  the main conversation orchestrates sequential subagent invocations, passing context between them

explanation: In Claude Code, the main conversation acts as the coordinator, spawning specialized subagents (workers) via the Agent tool. When the code analyzer's results need to feed into the documentation writer, the coordinator chains the subagents sequentially, passing relevant context from one to the next. This is the coordinator-subagent pattern with chaining.

why_others_wrong.a: Agent teams involve peer-to-peer communication between agents. In this scenario, the main conversation coordinates all work  --  workers don't communicate directly with each other.
why_others_wrong.b: While some subagents could run in parallel (the test generator might run alongside others), the described dependency between code analyzer and documentation writer requires sequential execution, which is the chaining pattern.
why_others_wrong.c: Subagents CANNOT spawn other subagents  --  this is a hard technical limitation. The code analyzer cannot spawn the documentation writer. Only the main conversation can invoke subagents.

doc_reference.quote: Coordinator = main conversation thread. Subagents = specialized workers spawned via Agent tool. Chaining: coordinator can invoke subagents in sequence, passing context between them.

question: A developer designs a hierarchical agent system where a manager subagent delegates subtasks to worker subagents. During testing, the worker subagents are never spawned. What explains this failure?

options.text: The manager subagent needs 'Agent' explicitly listed in its tools field to enable spawning
The worker subagent definitions are missing the required 'prompt' frontmatter field
Subagents cannot spawn other subagents  --  this is a technical limitation, not a configuration option
Hierarchical delegation requires enabling CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1

explanation: Subagents cannot spawn other subagents  --  this is a hard technical limitation. If your workflow requires nested delegation, you must either use Skills (which run in the caller's context) or chain subagents sequentially from the main conversation. The main conversation remains the single orchestration point, enforcing one-level-only delegation.

why_others_wrong.a: Even if Agent were listed in the manager's tools, subagents still cannot spawn other subagents. The limitation is architectural, not about tool availability.
why_others_wrong.b: While 'prompt' is not a required frontmatter field (only 'name' and 'description' are required), the real issue is that subagents fundamentally cannot spawn other subagents regardless of configuration.
why_others_wrong.d: Agent teams are a separate feature for peer-to-peer coordination between independent sessions. They don't enable hierarchical subagent nesting  --  that limitation is absolute.

doc_reference.quote: Subagents cannot spawn other subagents. If your workflow requires nested delegation, use Skills or chain subagents from the main conversation.

question: Your agent processes large codebases and frequently runs out of context. A subagent reads 6,100 tokens of files but only returns a 420-token summary to the main context. Which architectural benefit does this demonstrate?

options.text: Context isolation  --  the subagent works in its own context window and returns only the final text summary, keeping intermediate work out of the parent's context
Token compression  --  the subagent compresses file content before returning it
Prompt caching  --  the subagent's file reads are cached and reused without consuming parent context
Context editing  --  the subagent uses server-side context editing to remove large file contents before returning

explanation: Context isolation is the defining feature of subagents. Each subagent runs in its own context window  --  all intermediate work (file reads, tool outputs, reasoning) stays there. Only the subagent's final text response returns to the parent context. This provides significant context savings: in this example, 6,100 tokens of file reads are reduced to a 420-token summary.

why_others_wrong.b: There is no token compression mechanism. The savings come from isolation  --  the 6,100 tokens are never part of the parent context at all, not compressed into fewer tokens.
why_others_wrong.c: Prompt caching reduces costs for repeated content but doesn't reduce context consumption. The file reads would still be in context even if cached. Context isolation keeps them out entirely.
why_others_wrong.d: Context editing is a separate API feature for clearing tool results. The subagent's context savings come from architectural isolation  --  it has its own separate context window.

doc_reference.quote: Subagent reads 6,100 tokens of files in its own context. Only a 420-token summary returns to the main context. The large file reads stay entirely in the subagent's context window.

question: Your team needs multiple Claude instances that can message each other directly, share a task list, and self-coordinate without a single orchestration point. Which Claude Code feature provides this capability?

options.text: Multiple subagents spawned simultaneously with the background: true flag
Chaining subagents from the main conversation with context passing
Agent teams  --  experimental feature requiring CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1
Background subagents with isolation: worktree for parallel work

explanation: Agent teams are the feature designed for peer-to-peer agent coordination. Unlike subagents (which report back to a single parent), teammates can message each other directly via a shared messaging system, share a task list with self-coordination, and work as fully independent Claude Code sessions. This requires enabling the experimental flag CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1.

why_others_wrong.a: Multiple background subagents run concurrently but still report only to the parent. They cannot message each other or share a task list  --  they're isolated workers, not collaborating teammates.
why_others_wrong.b: Chaining subagents is sequential (one after another from the main conversation) and requires the main conversation as the coordination point. It doesn't provide direct inter-agent communication.
why_others_wrong.d: Background subagents with worktree isolation provide concurrent work with isolated git copies, but they still report to the parent conversation and cannot communicate with each other directly.

doc_reference.quote: Agent teams: Multiple agents working in parallel, communicating with each other. Different from subagents: coordinate across separate sessions. Requires CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1.

question: A background subagent encounters a tool call that was not pre-approved before launch. What happens?

options.text: The permission is automatically granted because background subagents bypass all permissions
The subagent terminates immediately with an error
The subagent pauses and escalates the permission request to the main conversation
The tool call is auto-denied, but the subagent continues working on the remaining task

explanation: Background subagents have their permissions pre-approved before launch. Any tool call that was not pre-approved is auto-denied, but the subagent continues working  --  it doesn't terminate. Similarly, clarifying questions fail but the subagent continues. If the subagent fails due to missing permissions, you can start a new foreground subagent with the same task to retry with interactive prompts.

why_others_wrong.a: Background subagents do NOT bypass all permissions. They have pre-approved permissions set before launch, and anything NOT pre-approved is auto-denied. This is a controlled security model.
why_others_wrong.b: The subagent does NOT terminate. It continues working despite the denied tool call. The denied call fails but the subagent adapts and tries alternative approaches.
why_others_wrong.c: Background subagents do NOT escalate permission requests to the main conversation. That's the behavior of foreground subagents. Background subagents simply auto-deny unapproved tool calls.

doc_reference.quote: Background: Run concurrently; pre-approve permissions upfront; auto-deny anything not pre-approved; clarifying questions fail but subagent continues.

question: A developer references 'Task tool' in their documentation for spawning subagents. During code review, you point out this is outdated. What is the correct current terminology and what backward compatibility exists?

options.text: It is now called the 'Agent' tool (renamed in v2.1.63), and existing Task(...) references still work as aliases
It is now called the 'Spawn' tool, and Task() references are no longer supported
It is now called the 'Worker' tool, and Task() references require a migration script to convert
The naming hasn't changed  --  'Task tool' is still the official name in the documentation

explanation: The Task tool was renamed to 'Agent' in Claude Code version 2.1.63. Existing Task(...) references in settings still work as aliases for backward compatibility. The documentation now refers to it as the 'Agent tool' and subagents are spawned through it. This renaming better reflects the tool's purpose of creating autonomous subagents.

why_others_wrong.b: The tool was renamed to 'Agent', not 'Spawn'. And Task() references continue to work as aliases  --  they were not removed.
why_others_wrong.c: The tool was renamed to 'Agent', not 'Worker'. No migration script is needed because Task() still works as an alias.
why_others_wrong.d: The naming DID change in v2.1.63. The official documentation now uses 'Agent tool' instead of 'Task tool'.

doc_reference.quote: Formerly called 'Task tool'  --  renamed to 'Agent' in version 2.1.63. Existing Task(...) references in settings still work as aliases.

question: A team member creates a subagent definition file with only a 'description' and 'prompt' field in the frontmatter, believing these are the two required fields. What correction should you make?

options.text: The required fields are 'description' and 'model'  --  name is auto-generated from the filename
The required fields are 'name' and 'description'  --  'prompt' is not a frontmatter field; the markdown body serves as the system prompt
The required fields are 'name', 'description', and 'tools'  --  all three must be present
All 16 frontmatter fields are required for a valid subagent definition

explanation: Only 'name' and 'description' are required frontmatter fields for subagent definitions. The system prompt is NOT set via a 'prompt' frontmatter field  --  it's the markdown body below the frontmatter. The body becomes the system prompt that the subagent receives. All other 14 fields are optional with sensible defaults.

why_others_wrong.a: Name is NOT auto-generated from the filename  --  it must be explicitly specified in the frontmatter. And 'model' is not required; it defaults to 'inherit' (the main conversation's model).
why_others_wrong.c: 'tools' is not required. When omitted, the subagent inherits ALL tools from the parent (including MCP tools). Only name and description are required.
why_others_wrong.d: Only 2 of the 16 fields are required (name and description). The remaining 14 are all optional.

doc_reference.quote: Only name and description are required. All 16 Supported Frontmatter Fields... name: Yes (required). description: Yes (required). [all others]: No.

question: You configure a subagent with 'tools: Read, Grep, Glob' in the frontmatter. A colleague asks whether the subagent can also access MCP tools from the parent session. What is the correct answer?

options.text: Yes  --  but only if the MCP server is also listed in the mcpServers frontmatter field
Yes  --  MCP tools are always available to subagents regardless of the tools field
No  --  when the tools field is explicitly set, only the listed tools are available. MCP tools are NOT inherited
No  --  subagents never have access to MCP tools under any configuration
