Processing may be slowed by demand and volume, causing more requests to expire. Batch results remain available for 29 days after creation.

why_others_wrong.a: API key expiration is unrelated to batch request expiration. The 24-hour window is per batch, not per API key.
why_others_wrong.c: Batch results are available for 29 days after creation, not 1 hour. The expiration refers to the processing window, not the result retrieval window.
why_others_wrong.d: Size limit violations would cause validation errors, not expiration. Expired requests are those not processed within the 24-hour window.

doc_reference.quote: Batches expire if processing does not complete within 24 hours. Batch results are available for 29 days after creation.

question: Your extraction pipeline processes long research papers with citations enabled and also uses prompt caching to reduce costs on repeated queries against the same document. How should you apply cache_control to optimize this pattern?

options.text: Apply cache_control to the citation blocks in the response so they are cached for subsequent queries
Apply cache_control to the top-level document content blocks in the request so the source document is cached
Apply cache_control to the system prompt only, since document content cannot be cached with citations
Enable global caching for the entire conversation by setting cache_control at the message level

explanation: When using citations with prompt caching, you should apply cache_control with type 'ephemeral' to your top-level document content blocks. The citation blocks in responses cannot be cached directly, but the source documents they reference can be. Subsequent requests using the same document benefit from the cached content.

why_others_wrong.a: Citation blocks generated in responses cannot be cached directly. Cache_control applies to input content, not output content.
why_others_wrong.c: Document content can absolutely be cached when using citations. The docs explicitly show applying cache_control to document blocks alongside citations.
why_others_wrong.d: cache_control is applied at the content block level, not the message level. There is no global caching toggle for entire conversations.

doc_reference.quote: The citation blocks generated in responses cannot be cached directly, but the source documents they reference can be cached. To optimize performance, apply cache_control to your top-level document content blocks.

question: Your extraction pipeline submits a batch with a custom_id for each request to correlate results with source documents. What are the constraints on the custom_id field?

options.text: Must be a UUID v4 format string
Must be 1 to 64 characters containing only alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and underscores
Must be a positive integer unique within the batch
Any string up to 256 characters including special characters and spaces

explanation: The custom_id must be 1 to 64 characters and match the pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$, meaning only alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and underscores are allowed. Each custom_id must be unique within the batch to allow correlating results with the original requests.

why_others_wrong.a: UUID v4 format is not required. The custom_id is a flexible string that can be any alphanumeric pattern with hyphens and underscores, up to 64 characters.
why_others_wrong.c: The custom_id is a string, not an integer. It supports alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and underscores.
why_others_wrong.d: The maximum length is 64 characters, not 256. Spaces and most special characters are not allowed.

doc_reference.quote: A unique custom_id for identifying the Messages request. Must be 1 to 64 characters and contain only alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and underscores (matching ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$).

question: Your extraction pipeline processes large contract documents using citations. Each document has been pre-split into logical sections like preamble, terms, conditions, and signatures. You want Claude to cite at the section level rather than at the sentence level. Which approach gives you this control?

options.text: Use plain text documents and set the chunking parameter to 'section' in the citations config
Use PDF documents since they naturally support section-level citations through headings
Use custom content documents where each section is a separate content block, giving you explicit control over citation granularity
Use plain text documents with section delimiter markers that the chunking algorithm will respect

explanation: Custom content documents provide explicit control over citation granularity. You define the content blocks, and no additional chunking is done. Each content block becomes a citable unit, so placing each contract section as a separate block means citations reference entire sections. Citations use content_block_location with 0-indexed block indices.

why_others_wrong.a: There is no chunking parameter in the citations config. Plain text documents are always chunked into sentences automatically.
why_others_wrong.b: PDF citations use page numbers, not headings or sections. There is no heading-based citation mechanism for PDFs.
why_others_wrong.d: The automatic chunking algorithm for plain text documents chunks into sentences regardless of delimiters. It does not recognize custom section markers.

doc_reference.quote: Custom content documents give you control over citation granularity. No additional chunking is done and chunks are provided to the model according to the content blocks provided.
